summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates Apple open-sourcing Swift and uses the occasion to address why codes of conduct matter in open source projects. He counters Jared Spool's argument that codes of conduct are mere placebos by drawing on insights about empathy from parenting and Deborah Tannen's research on communication styles. Atwood argues that creating a feeling of safety is itself valuable—people want to know someone stands with them, not just that problems will be solved. He announces that Discourse is adopting a code of conduct and urges other open source maintainers to do the same, emphasizing that such public statements of intent matter even if they can't guarantee perfect outcomes.
key_points: Apple open-sourcing Swift is a positive step for the developer community, continuing the trend of open source winning over closed source
Codes of conduct aren't just about preventing bad behavior—they're public statements that make people feel seen and supported
Engineers, especially male engineers, often rush to fix problems rather than first listening and providing emotional validation
Creating a feeling of safety is itself valuable and shouldn't be dismissed as mere placebo
Public statements of values matter even when we can't fully live up to them or guarantee perfect outcomes
money_quotes: A feeling of safety is, in fact, what many people are looking for. It's not the whole story by any means, but it's a very important starting point. An anchor.
The quickest way to turn a child's frustration into a screaming, explosive tantrum is to try to fix their problem for them.
Nobody on planet Earth is better at arguing to the death over a set of completely arbitrary, made up rules than the average programmer.

We all want the same thing. Comfort from someone who can tell us that the world is safe, the world is not out to get you, that bad things can happen to you but you'll still be OK because we will help you.
This should not be controversial. It should be common. And saying so matters.
If we as programmers can't come together through our collective shared horror over PHP, the Nickelback of programming languages, then clearly I have failed.
key_insight: Codes of conduct matter not because they can prevent all harm, but because publicly stating your values makes people feel seen and supported—and that feeling of safety is itself meaningful.
